figures.caption: Fig. 2: Detailed schematic of Prompt-Space Gating (PSG). PSG isolates and eliminates background-induced false positives before they enter the decoder. It formulates a localized gate decision map from the negative prompt embedding (Z−) to physically filter deceptive cues from the positive counterpart (Z+). The suppressed feature (Z̃+) is then fused with unactivated background cues and compensated via an anchored residual connection to yield the robust Conditioned Prompt Embedding (Z).
Table 3: Ablation on design choices. (I) conditioning paradigm, (II) PSG operator and residual anchoring, (III) PSG and token replacement, and (IV) LoRA injection strategy.

Table 4: Cross-dataset medical polyp segmentation. Models are trained on Kvasir-SEG and evaluated on CVC-ClinicDB and ETIS. Best overall are underlined and best SAM-based are boldfaced (IP-SAM belongs to both categories).

Fig. 4: Internal feature visualization of the Prompt-Space Gating (PSG) mechanism. Feature evolution from the baseline’s leakage (Col 2) to IP-SAM’s clean prediction (Col 3). Intermediate columns depict suppressed false positives (Col 4), complementary intrinsic prompts (Cols 5-6), gate decision map (Col 7), and feature energy adjustments (Col 8).
Table 1: Quantitative comparison (no-external-prompt). Best results are boldfaced and IP-SAM is highlighted in gray.

Fig. 1: Overall architecture of IP-SAM. Shifting the paradigm to prompt-space conditioning, IP-SAM utilizes a Self-Prompt Generator (SPG) to synthesize complementary intrinsic prompts (P±). These are projected into SAM2’s native manifold via the frozen prompt encoder. Prior to decoding, Prompt-Space Gating (PSG) leverages the negative embedding (Z−) to asymmetrically suppress deceptive cues in the positive embedding (Z+). The purified condition (Z) explicitly steers the mask decoder. For visual clarity, the optional auxiliary structural regularizer (ablated in § 4.3) is omitted.
